pages.text:
Kler + Nana gave us supper in the Swan Hotel. Very good. Conrad has been behaving very well.
	Sunday we packed up + left to watch at the Glad Mountain Gliding Club. We saw about 3 planes take off, [?spun] Fantastic vviews + [?ridge]. ([?we forgot [illegible]to])
	On for fish + chips in Abergavenny. On to Raglan Castle. Lovely sunshine.
	Then on to home.
__Tuesday [?7/6]th August__
	10 we left home + dropped Tinder off at kennels. The trafic + cars in the waiting area at the Channel Tunnel were lighter than usual.
__Wednesday 7th August__
	We camped next to a lake in a residential camp site, without paying at [?Humancourt] ** between [?Arras] + Cambrai.
	We walked in the woods in the evening.
	The lake was stipled with rain while we had breakfast + it hasn't stopped

raining during our trip on to St. Quentin.
	We visited the wrong big church first + then found the Basilica. Its onomous + vertical.

[Photo: labyrinth floor mosaic]

__Labyrinthe at St Quentin__

__Picardie__
__Catthedrals__

[Small map showing: Amiens, Beauvais, Noyon, Senlis, Laon, St Quentin, Soissons]

After leaving St. Quentin, Simon found a headlight was not working. We drove on to la Barbuise Campsite [illegible] (where we had stayed many years before) & had a walk over chalk fields! Near Troyes.
Thursday
  Simon can't mend the headlight because he's got a different one in the stores. In the end he discovered it was a broken fuse. We travelled south, stopping at an old church on the way which was closed. At Troyes we went to an Auchan shops, OK but a bit down market. We bought a la Reine pizza for lunch which was good, then carried on to Les Treilles Campsite [illegible] Montbard. Charles & Jenny arrived at 4 pm, so we had more tea & put up the tent. Then we went on a bike ride into Montbard. Eve bought athletes foot cream & Charles chose grey (wool) cotton for Eve to knit him a scarf. It's a good campsite - town & shops, restaurant & bike trail (Canal de Bourgogne) & free swimming.
Friday We went on a [illegible] 15 mile bike ride

La forge
de
Buffon

along the Canal & went to Buffon to see the Forge, about 1760, started by Buffon when he was 60. He spent his early life as a Naturalist, categorising birds. Then he built an amazing Forge

with beautiful architecture. Water-wheels powered 3 forges, tilt hammer, washing minerals, bellows. Part of the area is privately owned. We had bread & salami. Then we cycled to Aray+ the only food was delicious patisserie (prunes) We cycled on to Nuits sous Ravières, where we waited 30 min for a train back to Montbard. 17 miles
  Then we swam (too cold) Jenny & Eve also had to rig up the internet on their mobs (free, but we first had to download a film about Pampers.) Tricky. Simon & Eve had trouble with the lockers at the pool. Eve stood shivering because she cdn't work the codes without her glasses which were in the locker. (The following day I cdn't open the washing machine to get the

clothes out.)
  After a rest we went out to a glorious supper. Simon & Jenny had swordfish, avocado & grapefruit. We all had pork.
~~Friday~~ Saturday
  We packed up & drove to Abbey de Fontenay.

[photo: Galerie nord du cloître]
[photo: Notre-Dame de Fontenay (XIIIème siècle)]

It's one of the best places I've seen. Now lived in by the Montgolfier (Balloon descendants) family. The tilt hammer was
[photo: Vue aérienne]
invented he[re] in 1190 or 129[0]

On to Intermarché (actually we went back to ou[r

We arrived at __Ste Marie sur l'Ouche__ &
hung the washing & it dried. The weather is
now warmer & it's not threatening rain.
~~Saturday~~ Sunday
We cycled 27 miles, along the Canal de
Bourgogne away from Dijon to Eclus
Pont de Pany. We had lunch at Bryony's.
An amazing set-up next to the canal by
an English woman who spoke fluent French.
The food was just what we needed, but as
Charles said - English & not good. Simon had
ham & chips, Eve fish fingers & chips, Jenny
chicken nuggets & chips & Charles quiche &
chips - Not a tomato or green veg in sight!
The coffee was good. Jenny had forgotten her
sheepskin saddle cover & had a sore bum.
Eve had a sore bum even with the cover.
Limericks at Supper.

Monday
We set off before Jenny & Charles. They maybe
going to visit Paia. We went towards Sens
for a supermarket & the on to Provins.

We cycled past lots of Ecluses, lock-keepers houses 40[illegible]

[Photo: postcard glued in, captioned "En Bourgogne" — showing a lock-keeper's house beside a canal with mist on the water]

We reached an unusual campsite, a bit like
Center Parks, around gravel pits, Hermes Loisirs
also 'La Fontaine' [illegible] at Gouaix.
There's a bar & one of the pits is a
swimming pool. After tea we rode our
bikes around the site & had a drink at the [bar?]

It's very peaceful + green + pretty with the water. There's a remote control boat. I have shut the van doors so that Simon doesn't fall out when he falls asleep over his book.

Tuesday Simon woke me very early to roll over + look at the mist on the lake. It was very beautiful.
We drove on to Provins. It's only an hour's drive from Paris + has lots of ladies with lots of make-up. It's an old Mediaeval town, very pretty. We didn't see either of the sites (there are 4 main ones) but walked up the hill to Caesar's Tower (taken by the English in the 100 Year War who built a wall around it.).

It's octagon on a square base. We had a very good lunch – pizza + goat's

cheese + honey + crème brulee.

We continued north thru Reims to Bourget Commin. The campsite has a small indoor swimming pool + is friendly. There's a noisy family nearby with a child who doesn't stop crying wolf + screaming like a peacock. We read alot. __La Poste__

Wednesday
I got out of bed + into the pool + washed my hair. After breakfast we cycled. Simon has downloaded Russian military maps off the internet. These Russian maps have contours which are useful for planning routes that avoid steep hills. With the Russian maps there is another layer showing the Open Street Map which is a free world map generated by volunteers and this is good for footpaths and cycling routes. [?F]
We set off, shopping at Carrefour, much more cheerful than Auchan. We had lunch on the carpark. We went off on cycle ride along by fields + woods + canal, trying to stay level. There was an Italian [strikethrough: cemeti] cemetery + a British one. It was a [?sunger] ride. We got back for lunch. Then we read. We had

Supper in the restaurant of the camp-site.
There were nice people on the pitch near us — Eena & Christopher. He is organist at Freibourg. They have a smart, modern, yellow caravan.

Thursday
We set off & stopped at Carrefour, more cheapful than Auchan, lunch on the carpark. At about 2 pm we stopped at Abbeville, outside the old address of au pair Florence. Her mother invited us in. The family don't speak English. Florence now lives in Calais, but is on holiday in Corsica. Florence's mother is Genevieve. Her friend was there (a man) & grandmother & 2 nieces, Lucinda (15) & a 6 year old. Lucinda wants to visit England. Her mother died 3 years ago. The 6 year old is fun. The grandmother's first child died aged 1 in a bombardment. The second died of cancer 3 years ago. Very sad. The girls live in

Perpignan & are on holiday at their grandmother's.
Genevieve chatted to Florence on the phone, gave us a list of restaurants at Le Crotoy & booked us into her cousin's campsite at Novelles.
We reached the campsite & found a Chinese WW I Cemetry right next [to] it, so I took photos for Mark O'Neil.
__Aire Haies de Novelles__ *** very clean loos but unisex. :" Sheep & a donkey nearby but quiet campers. So odd that the Chinese Labour Corps were treated as prisoners, but have a magnificent Lutyens graveyard, [it] just like the Brits.

Friday
We went to get a map at the station, cycled to Le Crotoy. Not a very nice [bike] ride, next to a road. The tide was out all the time we were there (11.45–3) & it was interesting to watch people on the sand/mud in the bay.
We ate at Modo Modo. Very good food but

slow service. The first course looked like chipboard. __Mussels were__ Simon had mussels + I had fish soup. We had a nice selection of fish + lovely puttings - nougat ice cream + raspberry coulis.
     The village was over-packed with people in the market, but on our way back, we found it had all been cleared away.
     We had intended to take the little train home at 4, but as we left the restaurant it spotted with rain + we decided to cycle. The wind was with us. We left at 2.50 + got back to the van at 3.50.
     We set off towards Calais.
     The first campsite we found at Bien Assisse in Guines was full. They sent us on to __La Petite Source__ *** It was crowded + the people were quite rough but friendly. Simon went on a bike ride + Eve read. The drive to the campsite was

very bumpy. The Marais (bog) was around us. Lots of fishermen.
     A red fire engine arrived with lights flashing + we crowded around with the children to see the fire. But a nice man came up + explained that the red vans are in fact ambulances + it had been called - he mimed kicking - for domestic violence, so we were embarrassed to have been watching.
__Saturday__ August 17th 2013
     We packed up + switched off the gas + left with no rush. It took only 12 mins to the booking-in place + we caught a train 2 before our booked one. We motored up to Bucks. We lunched + walked down a path onto a common.
White Mark Farm   (no. 82)
Watlington (near care home) we looked at this good campsite for staying with William + Joseph in the autumn. Nr. Chvdan [?] Arms Pub.

pages.sketch_desc: A simple hand-drawn sketch of a church/spire shape on the right side of the page — appears to illustrate the story of the vicar carving a chalk spire shape on the hillside (the White Mark), showing a tall pointed spire above a rectangular base.
pages.text:
__Friday 27th September__
2.30. Simon, Eve + Tinder set off to meet William + Joseph for camping trips. We sat on Haddenham + Thame platform + William + Joseph got off the train directly opposite us, William carrying a scooter + an enormous pack.
We camped at White Mark Farm, [illegible] near the Carriers Arms Pub, Watlington.
We put up the tent + had bacon sandwiches. William put Joseph in a fleece inside his new sleeping bag.
     In the 1700s the vicar was miffed because his church didn't have a spire. So he carved one out of the chalk on the hillside. When he sat in his vicarage, the spire appeared over his church - the White Mark.
__Saturday 28th__
Charles arrived [sticker] at 8.35. for breakfast

after taking Jenny to her training day in London. She is working 5 days a week + training on Saturdays. Charles stayed for breakfast, lunch + supper.
     We went on quite a walk in the morning. The ground was mostly too rough for the scooter. William carried J. on his shoulders + Charles did sometimes. Unfortunately Tinder got into a field + chased some cows. Joseph had red crocs on which kept falling off. We passed a pond, Willow Pond, which had melted away. We were partly on the Ridgeway. Back at Watlington we went to a craft sale in the Memorial Hall + then had lunch at the Carriers Arms.
     Then we all had a long nap at the van. After tea Simon + William walked up the hill to the White Mark. The weather had been good, a bit of sun. It rained in the night.

captions: We saw a new housing estate & a steam train at Chinnor station.
pages.text:
Sunday September 29th 2013
    We packed up camp. Joseph played with Hannah in the next-door tent.
[postcard: CHINNOR and WHITELEAF CROSS]
We saw a new housing estate & a steam train at Chinnor station.

Joseph +
Baba

We cooked at [?Churton] & had lunch next to the railway line in the carpark. We dropped of William & Joseph at Princes Risborough & spent a lovely sunny afternoon at Upton House. Amazing earth-moving in the garden. Oil magnate. Lovely coloured stone. Worth coming back! Home via Gaydon & M.40.

October 18th Friday
3.0. We left for south of Guilford, Rudgwick Riverside Farm ****  We have come down for Mylo Ruby's christening at Church of the Good Shepherd near Woking. We arrived at 8pm. On the way we spent hours in a traffic jam on the way to a Datchett petrol station.
     Rain in the night  October 19th.
     We set off at 10 south on the [~~Thames~~] Downs Link bike path, right next to the campsite. We went for [?a] mile to see the bridge [=] which is the Downs Link symbol.  The inspector said the incline was too much for the Train, so they built a metal bridge (1865) over

the brick bridge. It existed for just nearly 100 years  Ahhh! Beeching!
It was a really nice ride. [?Used] at home in Surrey. At one point on the path we managed to collect nearly a bucket of sweet chestnuts.
     The ride was not easy. The path was muddy & slippery. But the autumn colours were beautiful & there were lovely views.

Surface was slicky + a turner tunnel is closed (pipistrelle bats) so we had to push up a "devil of a hill" according to a jogger to get round. One station, built for a Rev. Thurloe who gave his land cheap, is [sold] the only station to survive.

      We reached Cranleigh + it was raining. The 6 Horseshoe Pub allowed Tinder in, but there was no food because the publican's wife was in Labour + they'd gone off to hospital. The barman had not worked there for 3 years – he is now a primary school teacher. So I walked back + fetched food (turkey sausage baps) from outside the Co-op, + a cream bun. We were allowed to eat them in the pub over a pint of beer. We also bought batteries, chives + tomatoes.

      On the way back Tinder set ** off thru a wire fence next to a bridge + fell 15ft splat over the parget into a muddy stream. After much calling + encouragebt

is from Simon he came back.

      A lovely ride 13 miles. Supper at the van. The field [good?] with moonlight + it rained in the night.      1 owl – hoot

Sunday  I walked Tinder twice around the wet field. We got back in the van just before thunder + a terrific rain storm.

      We set off at 8.45. + got to the Church of the Good Shepherd outside Woking at 9.35. for the 10 o'clock christening. We came across Jane turning her car around + pointed out the right road to her. Also at the christening were the William Kirbys, Richard + Mary, John + Pamela. It was a good service, then we went back to a good lunch, chaos with lots of families, very enjoyable. We chatted to Louise + Dave, Dan's sister. We left at 2 + got home at 4.45.

      A lovely last trip of the year.

April  2014

  We had tea in the van at Lapworth
& walked along the canal.


May 16th.
2.45. Simon, Eve & Tinder left to drive
to meet William & Joseph near Felsted.
It was a huge field with short grass but
the man was not friendly & charged us for
2 pitches. Mean __Sewards Hall Farm__. * The
loos were very clean & the shower worked.
We didn't get there until 6.10 — the signing
of the A road was very bad & queues to get
onto the A14.
     We put Joseph to bed & had supper.
William & Joseph were awake quite a lot
but I slept like a log, having had Austin,
Flora & Betty to stay for 4 days while
Thomas, Naoomi & Walter were in Budapest.
Saturday May 17th
  We went along a disused railway line called
the Flitch Country Park. Very enjoyable. No

horrible fast riders. Before we left we had
to drive to a Halfords in Braintree
to buy a bike seat for Joseph — William
had forgotten his borrowed one — &
some gumpleads. Because William had
left the car doors open his battery had
gone flat.
     We had a delicious lunch at 47
near Raine, in the garden. Tinder ran
18 k & was pooped at the end, only
Trotting. Unfortunately Joseph fell asleep for
20 minutes in the bike seat on the way
home, so he didn't nap on getting back.
But his behaviour was very good
throughout the holiday. He watches DVDs
of Peppa Pig & demonstrates his prowess at
cycling backwards on his Ben 10 bike.
     We took it in turns to nap with the
afternoon. Joseph gave me a daisy, a
buttercup & a dandelion.
__Sunday 18th May__
  We walked throu winter wheat & William
showed us black weed which has to be
ploughed back in to get rid of it.

Then we went to lunch with Angela Sills. She has a gorgeous house, moated, + garden. Her husband, Tim + sons, James [?Robert] (Skill) [?Robert] Michael (Durham 20) were at a Young Farmer Agricultural Show. We walked around the Farm + Joseph sat on a digger + a tractor. Her men returned at 2.30. by which time William + Joseph had set off back to London. Lovely Green Man + Sungoddes in her garden + plans.

   3.45. We drove south down the hills into the Thames Estuary + camp at [?At] Bulphan [?Ha] Hall Farm. 2 crashes. The campsite is in a pretty area. It's very green. More warm sunshine.

Monday 19th May
   We drove into Docklands. It was interesting to view London arriving from the east. Poor Tinder was trapped in the loo while we looked at the Excel exhibition where Swafer had a stand with Chad on it. Simon + I

looked around + Simon liked a round table with laminated wood showing by Jason Heap who lives on the Isle of Wight. He met him. I went back to the van + read. We had lunch + set off north, stopping for tea on the way. We got home + Simon mowed the lawn before the church Book Club came.

Friday June 6th.
   3.10. Simon, Eve + Tinder set off for Charles'. 2 flats at [?Suns] Tew. There we arrived just before William + Bryony, Joseph + Seb. Then John came. Finally Charles, who later collected Jenny. Jenny had cooked a delicious big lasagna + salad + W + B mortgaged the house + brought me a yummy cake from Conditor + Cooke. I had lovely presents. Sensible people fell into bed + Charles + Jenny + John went off to the pub. Poor Charles had had an exam that day.
June 7th. I was fast asleep + Simon shaving when Bryony arrived at 8 with the Z boys

They were very patient while John (who was leaving early to go back to Swansea) climbed into bed with us & we had tea, dressed & had breakfast. We & joined us for breakfast. It was so nice to have a meeting place for visitors.
        They left. Charles & Jenny got up. Jenny was fed up at having another driving lesson. We chatted. We left for Conta's house at Hinton Parva. We looked at the church next door. St. Swithins. Conta had paid £500 to prevent concrete being put over all the stones by the new warden.
        Iona & Theo were studying for their second year medical exams. Really too nice a day to study. Conta was annoyed that Wanda's gymkana was cancelled because lightning had been forecast. Wanda is at C.V.L.C. Mike was in Wales golfing with a friend. Tinder had a wild run around with Conta's dogs. Iona wonders if he could breed with Willow.

captions: ENGLISH HERITAGE STONEHENGE — Step into England's story
pages.text:
We got in for free with EH/NT membership. Otherwise— family £39

[glued-in Stonehenge English Heritage ticket: "ENGLISH HERITAGE STONEHENGE / Step into England's story / Join English Heritage FOR MORE GREAT DAYS OUT / Web Member Adult / Tour Date: 08 Jun 2014 09.00 / Valid on day of purchase only"]

        On to __Ashdown House__, a hunting lodge built in 1662 by Lord Craven (nouveau riche Cavalier) for his girl-friend Elizabeth, daughter of James I, sister of Charles I & wife of King of Bohemia? who had lost his throne. She loved hunting. You could view the land for miles around at the top of the house, cupola balcony.

The house is tenanted. It is tidy with 3 reception rooms + 8 bedrooms all closed. The 3.15. tour took us up the staircase, very wide, past paintings given to Craven by Elizabeth, 10 of her children. Excellent guide called Nicola. There's a long, underground passage out from the cellar because of 'nervousness' after the Civil War. The avenues are a mile long.
    We camped at a really nice site, Orcheston village, Stonehenge Touring Campsite* [?★★★] very welcoming, clean, friendly, library. There was a trailer serving grilled food, so we had chips with our chicken salad.
Sunday
9.0 We had first-entry tickets for Stonehenge. It was just as well because when we came to leave (10.30.) it was really crowded. The new visitors' centre, opened in the last 12 months, is very good. Light + airy. The exhibits capture the scale of Stonehenge. The big stones are sareen stones, dragged

captions: George Herbert, West Front, Salisbury Cath[edral]
pages.text:
19 miles from Marlborough. The smaller, blue stones came 150 miles by water + land, from Prestelli in Wales.

[photo: stone statue on cathedral facade]

George Herbert, West Front, Salisbury Cath[edral]

captions: Bemerton Church Window - Ferrar and Herbert
pages.text:
On to Bemerton. We are reading "Music at Midnight by Drury, about George Herbert, for the church book club. The village has huge water meadows, which used to be flooded every winter to keep them fertile. It felt abit like Amiens. We met an old lady who was friendly & told us about the George Herbert festival July 11". (John's graduation.)
    On to the campsite.... which couldn't have us. A farm campsite *
    As dusk fell there was a raucous noise from the oak tree next to us. Screeching, murder, mayhem. It went on for ages Then 2 owls flew swooping away. When they came back there was more noise. It went on & on thru the night as they fed

their young.
    Finally Simon got an answer on the campsite telephone. The husband. He said we shd put £15 in the box. Simon said he'd see if he had the change. It seemed grossly expensive; all we had was mown grass & owls. So we left £10.
    On to Farnham where Simon dropped me off & went on to a Canna meeting with Richard. I shopped & went to see Jane's new house. It's lovely & light. She watches the TV, Wimbledon, from behind a silk parasol because of the sun's glare.
    We met up with Mary & Richard & Hattie & Piers for a good pub lunch. Simon drove home with Tinder, after leaving me at the station to visit Kate at Woking & then William at Waterloo.

Friday June 27th.
	Simon, Eve + Tinder left just after 6. We arrived about 7.45 at Great Rissington, Ashespring **** & filled with water. There's a beautiful view to the west, grey clouds, alas, not an encouraging sunset for the next day. Loos in shower room, 20f them. Gnomey flowers, but nice. Horses. Tinder is behaving better. ~~Sat~~ At 9.30, just as we put our coats on for a walk, it started to rain. Lots of bird paths.
	We went thro fields, lovely church with a 1938 wall-hanging, embroidered with a living map of the village. Fantastic Cotswold houses, rich Chelsea tractor, beautiful gardens. Idyllic. It rained more as we got back to the van.
	Charles + Jenny arrived at 11.20. We walked back up to the Lamb pub to book supper. We had a drink too. Tinder was allowed in.

After lunch we ~~drove~~ walked into Bourton-on-the-Water. Very pretty. The aim of the trip was to have an icecream, which we did. It rained heavily & we ate them under a tree & then hurried back to Charles' car & back to the van for a cup of tea.
	Before supper we walked & then went to the Lamb, where we ate in the bar so that Tinder could be with us.
__Sunday__
	Simon & Eve set off on a walk before Charles & Jenny arrived at 11. It was up hill & down dale & we went through heavy undergrowth & oozing black swampy mud & got back in time. ~~lunch~~. Then as we sat we heard a thunderous farty noise & Charles & Jenny arrived wearing helmets in the sublime folly of the Morgan 3 wheeler. There followed trips in the

car (wonderful) & hours of car Talk.
I [?al] almost finished the pink sweater that I was knitting for Flora's birthday.
lunch, then another lovely 3½ mile walk.
I think Great Rissington has the most fabulous group of Cotswold House that I've seen. As Jenny says, you wouldn't dare let the outside of your house appear bad if you lived there.

	Then tea, then we all left. The first time we had to stop on the return journey was at the Green Man red light in Harborne.

August 19th 2014.	John's birthday.
He has just completed his first 2 weeks' work in Swansea. Charles has driven back safely from Noux in his tub-on-wheels. Jenny has brought Helen [back] by plane from Noux. Thomas + family have had a week in Germany with Annie + a week in a cabin in France. William + Bryony are in London. Peter + family have left for Chhattrapati's 50th birthday in Calcutta.
	Over tea in bed, Simon decided to leave today evening instead of tomorrow morning. I managed to get to sewing as well as pack up. We set off at 6.10 pm. Two campsites refused us so I rang Kate when a road sign said "Woking" + left a message. There was a house/dog sitter. We slept on the drive + left at 7.20.

Wed Aug 20th
We moved to a services for breakfast + got to the Channel Tunnel early + the train was 50 minutes late. We've arrived at __les Haies de Nolettes__ *** where we've been before. There's a Chinese WW1 cemetery.
	We cycled to the cemetery + to the next village with 2 stations, one for modern, one for old trains. It rained in the night.
Noyelles sur Mer.

__Thursday__
    We went west, though Picardy into Normany, stopping at a roadside restaurant a few miles east of Caen. Tomorrow we will reach Britanny. xx[?x+x]
    Tonight we have stopped at __Camping l'Aurore__ Cherrueix. The shower works by just pulling on a chain. We are 10 k west of Mont St. Michel. I could not get the camp password to work on my iphone for wi-fi. So I have paid £1-99 for a day of data-roaming... I think.
    __Friday__
    Cyclists camp here with tents, [?Dutch] who have cycled 300 miles on Voies Vertes + Brits who got off the boat at St. Malo. We leave + go past 4 or 5 windmills, almost polder country, + thatched houses.
    Sign posts are in 2 languages. We follow a sign to a chapel, St. [illegible] + had lunch, Lisorno

captions: Avec l'assurance de nos priéres près de Saint-Yves
pages.text:
[photo/postcard glued in: statue of three figures with caption "Avec l'assurance de nos priéres près de Saint-Yves"]

Tréguier, Brittany. St. Yves, patron saint of lawyers, became unpopular when he judged in favour of a poor man.

Van walked, then had trouble finding the main [?D]. Stopped at Super-U. [?I've moved] the van to try to get out of the rain.

On to Tréguier, very pleasant town on a hill side. A cloister at the Cathedral St. Tugdal. We bought Far Breton in the cake shop in honours of Fatana who gave me the recipe many years ago. After walking around we parked down below the town on the waterside, it's high tide in a tidal estuary. We have a cup of tea.

     We go out for another walk, this time along the Estuary, crossing back + forth across 2 bridges. Then we go into the town, trying to trace the course of the old railway, but it's guesswork. Down by the modern harbour we look in a second hand bookshop + buy a book on coach travel for Eve + Daumier's medical people for John

     Back at the van we had a delicious suppes. Paimpol cider, cheese, pâté, Far Breton + Westfield Rd raspberries,

All the vans parked with us along the Estuary (24) seem to be built to prevent enjoyment of the fabulous view up + down the river. The backs are all blocked in + people swivel their front chairs to face backwards while they eat. So only we could enjoy the view.
__Saturday__
We watched the tide go out in the evening. The river was full again at breakfast time. Still no-one else enjoying the glorious view. We walked [?looking at rocks as to—]
We drove north + soon came to [?Plounhinach] [?Clo—]
We wanted to walk along the Sentier des Douaniers but parking was difficult in the first bay we came to. Then we found a less touristy bay + hoped our parking space was allowed.
     Simon had a huge bowl of moules marinières I had a galettes with lots of things in it (buckwheat thin pancake.) Then we had good coffee.
     The weather is warm enough if I wear a sweater + coat, quite a bit of sunshine. The rocks in this poking-up bit of Brittany are amazing, very like Mahabalipuram. Pink granite worn away in parts. Balancing.

captions: Pavé breton (coffee biscuit wrapper — black + white quartered, Symbols on the Breton Flag)
pages.text:
The tide is out during lunch - huge mud flats + marinas. The houses are modern + expensive, or old + expensive. Some lovely gardens, but many with no gardens which shows they must be holiday houses. A prosperous area. Mostly French people.
Everytime we tried to set out along the footpath it seemed to wind back to the sea. Then we were distracted by the French air force practising their Red Arrows. Amazing.
   So we gave up on the walk + went back to the van.
Symbols on the     [glued-in Pavé breton coffee biscuit packet]
Breton Flag
coffee biscuit.
(black + white quartered)
Alot of the church spires are cheap-looking. Even the modern houses have huge stone surrounds to doors + windows which makes them look nice + fit in. Lots of ever-green trees, hydrangeas, agapanthus in gardens + globe artichokes in fields.

We camped near [?Six-Pleeter] ([?al Gieves])
The name of the campsite owner is Gwenaelle Thomas. It must be a Breton name. In 1950 2 mill people spoke Breton. Now its only 100,000. It is the only Celtic language not supported by the gov'. It only cost €8.80 + is a lovely campsite.
__Camping à la ferme__   * * * * *   So dark at night that Simon saw a shooting star + opened his constellations app on his mob. Very friendly woman who gave us a bag of veg for free. We had a delicious supper with pâté, cheese + choc. + almond croissants
   Sunday
Simon took ages to fill the van with the presser tap.
New German word - Grablegung - The Entombment
We drove to look at Enclosed Parishes, peculiar to this region, made possible by wealth from the linen trade 16 17 18[C]. Like cathedral closes, the parish churches + bone chapels were enclosed by a wall.

Eve's favourite cider.

The bone chapels/mausoleums were not big enough, so after a few years bodies were removed. Bones were put in an ossuary + heads in a casket. We saw an impressive oak entombment + [strikethrough] lots of glittery carvings at St. Thégonnec. The whole thing reminded me a bit to the trip to the Hindu Temple commemorating the dead with Nana, Peter + Conrad. Extraordinary. One of the statues had 2 horses doing a pooh.

On TR to __St. Gwinilian__, another enclosed parish. The art of the church more welcoming. Some sort of thing most odd. This area of Brittany has about 20+ parishes of the same ilk. Lovely oak carvings on organ loft + baptistery.

   We drove on to __Etel__, a long harbour, fed by a river. The campsite '__Camping de la Falaise__ ***' allowed us to choose the pitch lovely views of grass-covered dunes + distant town + + boat masts. Distant island [strikethrough] Groix. The sea has different colours in diffrent directions. It started to rain as Simon went off to the loo + we made the bed. It rained off + on thro the night.

__Monday__
   Dreadful-looking weather when we woke up. But when I left the van — to brush teeth, use loo + empty coffee pot, I found it wasn't too bad, quite warm.
   First stop at standing stones, 1130 stones at Kerzetho (Alignments). I'm touching stones put there 3800 years ago [strikethrough: C] 5800 years ago!
   On to the Dolmen of Crucuno,

11 stones supporting a table, making a chamber. A long entry chamber was destroyed 100 years ago to build a barn (now for sale) next to it. Built 4000 years ago + we've been married 40 years.
     For 50 years I have muddled Karnak (I saw in Syria or Jordan, tall columns) with Carnac here in France. Simon has pointed out the K. There is also a Krac here.
     On to the Dolmen of Mané-Groch. This one had side-chambers. Just long enough to sleep in ? or store bones? An entry passage, central area (10 + 10ft) + 4 side chambers off. Roof had fallen in on central one?
     Same size chambers as Grimes Graves, Norfolk. It's raining such a pity otherwise we could have biked to these little sites.
     Jesus on the way side + church stone crosses always has straight legs + toes crossed over each other.
     It rained more so we stopped at a Super U + shopped. Hair stuff good, slides, etc.

captions: St. Thégonnec, Renaissance Ossuary Chapel + entombment in oak in crypt.
pages.text: St. Thégonnec, Renaissance Ossuary Chapel + entombment in oak in crypt.

captions: Big polished axes discovered at Carnac, Alpine jadite, Neolithic, 4500-4000 av. JC.
pages.text:
[Photo of polished stone axes arranged in a fan pattern; caption written vertically alongside photo:] Big polished axes discovered at Carnac, Alpine jadite, Neolithic, 4500-4000 av. JC.

After lunch we walked about ¼ mile to the Museum of Prehistory. It was pouring as we walked back to [continued on right page] the van & we got very wet. Thank goodness [continued on right page] for dry clothes & a cup of tea.
The museum had an interesting silent film from di[fferent/continued on right page]

captions: [?Table Rutual], Pierres Plates, Menhir Brisé, Kercadoret dolmen
pages.text:
[Continuation of text from left page, left margin:] [illegible] [illegible] [illegible]

[Postcard glued in: LOCMARIAQUER - Sites mégalithiques, showing multiple megalithic sites; caption written vertically on right margin:] [?Table Rutual], Pierres Plates, Menhir Brisé, Kercadoret dolmen

1925. The wife of one of the archeologists bought a 16 mm camera. She & the whole family worked at arch!. Her husband was shot at the end of the war because he didn't join the [?French] army (he fought wi. Germans)

Most of the museum was rather heavy-going because our French wasn't good enough. I preferred the Stonehenge museum. If it rains less tomorrow we'll go on a guided tour.
  We found __campsite of the white sand__ — a lovely beach. Crowded. It poured & poured rain. After supper there was an amazing rainbow as the sun came out & the sky cleared. We went for a lovely walk along the beach — a beautiful pink lilac sky. Then we read.
__Tuesday__
Sand everywhere, including in bed. Euch. Feet sticky. I had a shower & bravely washed my hair. Sunshine. It dried quickly.
  At 11.30 we started the English tour of the most westerly of the Carnac sites & the heavens kept opening. Cold & wet. Cold & wet. We had a good guide.
  We looked at the enclosure afterwards [illegible]

where farm houses had been built in among the stones, using the stones. There are nearly 3,000 stones left. There used to be double that number. It is reckoned that every house around has a stone in from Carnac. They stood up right [—] local stones [and] an enclosure at the end for worship in each of the 3 sites. Celtic words used. Celts arrived 600 BC (Stones 6,000 BC).
Men = stone.
Men-hir: high Stone
Dol-men: stone table.
Menhirs are sites of the living.
Afterwards back in van, set off east. Sunny lunch in lay by. Pick blackberries.

On + we picked black-berries at lunch time. Simon sat in the sun.
On arrival at [?Flatter] __Malestroit__ municipal campsite * * * * *
we wandered into town on our bikes, chained them infront of the church, looked aroun[d] the town. + shopped.

[Glued-in photo: standing stones at Carnac at sunset, captioned "ALIGNEMENTS DE CARNAC"]
[Glued-in ticket: CARNAC, Alignements de / VCI TR CARNAC VISITE CONF INDIV / VCI Adulte - Anglais / le 26/08 a 11:30 / Menec ouest / Valable le 26/08/2014 / 5.00 EUR / Vendu le 26/08/2014 a 11:03 NI ECHANGE NI REMBOURSE / C213/PATI 240690052393]

Simon bought a purse, Eve bought gloves. We went back to the van (over the bridge across the river) + returned to town to have supper at the Grain of Salt. At 8.30. we went to a fabulous organ concert in the Church by Prof Mickael Gaborieau. Really amazing. There was a video link so we could see his hands, feet + the music clearly.

__Wednesday__ It's not raining but the weather didn't look hopeful. We had originally thought of spending 2 nights here, but with rain forecast for the afternoon, we moved out of the camp + set off on a bike ride. The campowner's wife said it wouldn't rain. We cycled N.W along the Brest-Nantes Canal. Then William rang to say his passport had been in the stolen Knapsack + he needed a new one for Majorca next week + wanted our passport details. We reached St. Andrew of the Rock (lovely- well-cared for church) + had coffee + cycled home, a bit of a gentle uphill, along the voie-verte - disused railway line. Lunch back in Malestroit. We didn't expect much when the mothy bar man said only sandwich or kebab, but the kebab turned out to be a big hamburger sandwich w/ chicken stew + salad - rather good.

back at the van we texted William with the details, had coffee & left. Onto __[?Bréon] en [?Ail]__, we walked around before bed. There seemed to be either gypsies or poor people on the campsite. One woman had a washing machine + tumble drier on a huge wooden pallette outside. Rain, rain. Mud but no litter but all their rubbish was in bags by the washrooms. Most odd.

__Thursday__
More rain, gradually clearing during the day. We drove thro or past [?Mencon], [?Cartrouges], the round Argenten to the Chambois + looked at the town + newly laid wreaths. 20 years ago we met an American on a beach in Devon (1944 for Juno? beach) now we see French memorials after 70 years, August 2014. On Aug 19-22 the Amer, Canadians, French, Brits & Poles closed the Falaise rocket. The gap was 30k, then down

to 3 k while attacked by Poles on Hill 262 (ht) the Polish Hills, the Germans fled along the Corridor of Death. Horses (only 30% of the German army was modernised) +

[glued-in postcard/leaflet — printed text:]
__MONTORMEL__
It's raining in Montormel
And yet, it's summer ;
Heaven is weeping over events former.
It's raining in Montormel...

Il pleut sur Montormel
Et pourtant c'est l'été
Le ciel pleure le passé
Il pleut sur Montormel...

[handwritten caption:] Montormel Memorial

men rotted & were bull-dozed into pits by the Brits: 100,000 Germans, 50,000 escaped, 40,000 prisoners, 10,000 killed. Flies & smell were terrible. Land didn't recover until 1954.

    We shopped at le Clerke & went on [?**RS] to __bigne le Foret__ to camp at __St. Paul__, It's a very pretty valley. You could walk up into the town. A "trout stream" (8fat) runs thro the camp Good showers. About 2½ hours from the Channel Tunnel. It rained in the night. A beautiful old town. I'd like to walk here.

    __Friday__
    We left at 8.35. For Charleval + petrol [?Salon] Fortunately the machine took a card. We reached the train on time & had lunch in the train. On to Whittlesey St., where I knocked very loudly on the door. We brought pudding with us.

    __Saturday 30th August.__
    After a night & lunch with the William Kirby we set off for [?Tinds] on the way home.

__Saturday Oct 3[rd] 4th__
    Thomas is in India. Naomi's about to have a baby. At 11.30 we set off with Austin to __Wolverley__ to camp for the night & go on a bike ride.
    We found a pitch where we can see the Playground – __Wolverley__ **** v.clean loos. In the afternoon we walked around the village & saw the outside of the church Austin played with a girl called Ruby who was also here with her grandparents Lovely sunshine after a rainy morning. The night was freezing cold. Austin disappeared in his sleeping bag.

    __Sunday Oct 5th.__
    We set off at 9.15 on the bike ride & got back at 2.45. We cycled to Kidder & had coffee. Then on to Stourport. V.attractive Tontine Hotel & appartments for sale. Georgian, from start of canal hotels. Built by Brindley, it was a hamlet calls

called Mitton, renamed by Brindley. But nowadays v. cheap fairground, incomprehensible language "... Bab!" v. poor [?pess] people. We bought lunch for £4.95 - a roast. Inedible chicken, teeth bounced as if chewing car tyre. Spat it out & have ready in doggy bag for Trader's supper. As bad as a meal near Garsford or the English lunch on the French canal.
	Austin (6) was BRILLIANT on the 15m like ride.
	The campsite used to be an American WW2 army hospital. Out of 10,000 patients only 4 died.
	Back to the camp for [?tea/teas] then set off home.

Oct 11th 2014 Saturday
	8.20. Simon, Eve & Tinder left for

Mandarin - stone, slate, granite for lights at Trumpington. Gorgeous stones for bathrooms & kitchens. Eve telephoned Kate & Bryony to tell them about it because they are putting in bathrooms & kitchens [illegible]

at the farm. There is a showroom at Monmouth.
	Then on to Annette who took us out to lunch at Auberge. Big John's son is getting married. We had chicken/sausage (Eve) & Guinea fowl (Annette & Simon). Delicious. And crème brulée, raspberry sorbet & chocolate mousse.
	Then we went to tea with John & Pamela. Money shortage.
	On to __Kenninghall__ * * * * * South Lodge.
	Tiny. Shed for washroom. Good.
	Thunder, lightening, rain. The owner told us there'd be a concert of local music talent at the church at 7.30. We didn't really want to go out. But we did & it was super - David Knier played guitar & sang, John Loker played the mouth organ. They were terrific.
	The pub landlord also played the mouth organ. There is an arrangement that the

pub can go to the church to pray & the church can go to the pub – the Red lion to pee. The food is supposed to be very good.
    A freezing (almost) cold night. It's really too cold to camp now. Trouble with the room heater.

[glued-in ticket: RoofAid presents Raise the Roof! St Mary's Church, Kenninghall Saturday 11th October 2014 Doors open 7:00pm for 7:30pm Admit One: £5 in advance, £6 on the door Ticket No: 142]

Sunday
    Visited St. Mary's South Lopham. 5 tiered tower, Norman. 900 year old chest (dug out). The elephant & castle on the now end is from the Beaufort family. 6 Betts on the

war memorial & 2 families with 5 names. Now sun breaking through the fog, so we might walk in Thetford Forest.
    There's now a bridge near the church to replace what used to be a ford between 2 springs, one of R. Ouse, one of R. Waveney. When there's a flood, Norfolk becomes an island.
    We drove on to woods & walked next to the wood on a track with fields on one side, growing onions & turnips? Motorcycles passed. It was a super walk with Tinder. Then back into the van & back to B'ham for tea.
